text.content:
Phase 9 Endings Overview

Phase 9 ends at the Lower Route Fork (Location 14). The party has, by this point, either:

  • Opened the Black Ledger Gate cleanly (any of Methods A–E without major Backlash) and walked through to the Fork.
  • Forced or bypassed the Gate and is at the Fork under various states of pursuit or invisibility.
  • Decided not to cross the Gate — possibly because they have been driven back, possibly because they have decided to retreat with what they have.

The Phase 9 ending is structured around eight outcome packages (A–H). Each describes a different combination of method used, clock state, NPC outcomes, and Phase 10 implications.

Reading the ending

The GM should:

  1. Read the closing read-aloud at the Lower Route Fork (next page).
  2. Hand out Handout 8 (Lower Route Marks) if not already given.
  3. Pause. Let the table sit with it.
  4. Walk through any final per-PC moments (PC threads).
  5. Choose the appropriate outcome package (A–H) and read its summary aloud.
  6. End the session.

What carries forward to Phase 10

  • Black Ledger Gate method used. Affects Phase 10's opening reception.
  • Under-Road Alert Clock final value.
  • Record Destruction Clock final value. Determines how much evidence the party has.
  • Breath and Stone Clock final value. Determines whether the waystation is still intact behind them.
  • Which route was chosen at the Fork. Recovery, Escort, Supply, or Retreat.
  • Companion NPC status. Haskur, Kethren, Korre, Merrit, Savax — alive, dead, with the party, with the route.
  • Witnessed PCs. Any PCs the Tally Sentinel pressed its tablet against carry a route-mark into Phase 10.

Closing Read-Aloud — The Lower Route Fork

Read this when the party has crossed (or bypassed) the Black Ledger Gate and stands in the Lower Route Fork chamber.

The gate has closed behind you.

It closed without sound, or with the sound of stone remembering. You are not sure which. The slab descended into its frame the way a careful hand sets a tile back into its place, and now you stand on the far side of it, in a chamber smaller than the one before, lit by no lamps, with three corridors leading away.

Above each corridor, a route mark cut into the lintel. To your left, the recovery sign. Straight ahead, Kethren's courier mark paired with the seven cuts. To your right, the cargo-tally with the seed-and-wax indicators.

The air here is colder than the waystation behind you. Not colder by much — perhaps five degrees, perhaps less. Enough to notice.

You can feel, beneath your feet, the cistern slit still flowing. The vibration of slow water through old stone. You cannot hear it. The chamber's acoustics swallow what it makes.

You are carrying things you did not have an hour ago. A wax impression of a registry column. A stone tag from a crate. A folded paper scrap that says one line on each side. A wax strip that lists six standing instructions. Perhaps more. Perhaps a person, sitting on a stone bench in a cell behind you, or walking quietly at the rear of your group now, dressed in the only clothing she has worn for three weeks.

Above you, the world you came from keeps its accounts. The Aerdy Commercial-Court Network has its ledgers. The Yeomanry Council has its papers. The Watch has its files. The Brass Crow has its receipts.

Below, in three directions, something has been keeping you.

The marks on the lintels are not signs to a destination. They are categories. Recovery. Escort. Supply. The route does not point at places. It points at procedures.

You stand at the threshold of three corridors. The route does not care which you choose. It will administer whichever path you take.

You think, distantly: they did not build a road beneath the world.

They built a way to decide who was allowed to matter below it.

Beats to land at the table

  • The phrase “No sail. No road.” should be in everyone's mind. The route is the third option that phrase implied; the party has now walked it.
  • The party is carrying real, tangible artifacts — the GM should list aloud (or have the table list) the handouts and items they have in their packs. The weight should feel real.
  • The closing line should land alone. Pause before reading it. After reading it, do not speak. Let one of the players speak first.

After the read-aloud

Hand out Handout 8 (Lower Route Marks) if not yet given. Hand out Handout 9 (Black Ledger Gate Inscription) if the party took an impression. Let the table sit with the artifacts.

Then proceed to the outcome package the party's play has earned.

Outcome Packages

Choose the package that matches play. The GM should internalise the beats and let them shape Phase 10's opening narration.

Outcome A — Quiet Witness

Triggers: Gate opened by Method A (Witness), B (Kethren), or E (False Category) without alarm. Alert Clock 0–2. Record Destruction Clock 0–2.

Summary: The party has crossed the gate cleanly and preserved most or all of the evidence. The waystation behind them is undisturbed. The route does not know anything unusual happened — or, more precisely, does not know yet. An audit may eventually catch the irregularity, but not for weeks.

Phase 10 opens with: stealth, strong evidence, and a route that has not been alerted. The party arrives at the next waystation as expected route arrivals.

Outcome B — Evidence Rescued, Alarm Raised

Triggers: Gate opened cleanly but Alert Clock 3–4 during the phase. Record Destruction Clock 0–3.

Summary: The party has the evidence they came for. The route knows they were here. Pursuit may not be in active motion yet, but the next waystation has been informed; the party's surface-side faces are circulating below.

Phase 10 opens with: active pursuit at the next waystation, or prepared defenses. The party still has their evidence; they cannot rely on the route being unaware.

Outcome C — Haskur Trail Followed

Triggers: Party chose the Recovery Route (left-hand corridor). May combine with any clean opening.

Summary: The party is pursuing Haskur's transfer, whether to recover him alive, confirm his death, or interrupt his processing. Phase 10 begins by following the recovery route down to a specialised processing waystation.

Phase 10 opens by: tracking Haskur or his substituted witness; encountering route personnel specialised in asset processing; potentially confronting whoever (or whatever) actually processes recovered assets.

Outcome D — Kethren Route Followed

Triggers: Party chose the Escort Route (centre corridor). Best for parties interested in route culture.

Summary: The party walks the route's main administrative artery. Phase 10 begins by encountering the route's middle administrative tier — couriers, secondary wardens, occasional travelers. The deeper-authority mark appears more frequently along this route.

Phase 10 opens by: learning route culture, the fear of recall in route personnel, how couriers like Kethren live and operate; building the party's understanding of the route without yet meeting its deepest figure.

Outcome E — Supply Route Followed

Triggers: Party chose the Supply Route (right-hand corridor). Best for parties interested in the operation's mechanics.

Summary: The party follows the cargo route. Blooming Rot samples and recovered crates go this way; financial paper trails terminate down this route. Phase 10 reveals the operational mechanics — how recovery infrastructure actually works in practice.

Phase 10 opens by: walking past more crates than they will be comfortable with; finding inventories that connect to specific surface farms, surface families, surface bonded claims; meeting personnel who handle things, not people.

Outcome F — Forced Gate

Triggers: Black Ledger Gate opened by Method F (Force). Alert Clock advances +2; Breath and Stone +2. Tally Sentinel woken.

Summary: The party broke the gate. The route knows. The next waystation has received an emergency signal. The party may have one or more PCs witnessed by the Tally Sentinel.

Phase 10 opens under: alarm. Wardens at the next station are preparing correction. The party is not just visitors; they are incidents. Encounters will be more lethal, more procedural, less negotiable.

Outcome G — Retreat with Proof

Triggers: Party chose not to cross the Black Ledger Gate; they retreat to the surface with whatever evidence they recovered.

Summary: The party brings the Witness Tally Fragment, the Recovery Crate Tag, the Furnace Scrap, and/or other handouts back up the stair to Tarnsmere and out. They are alive. They are carrying enough to change the surface investigation entirely. The route, behind them, has time to relocate, close, or repair.

Caelith's warning: “Proof has weight only if someone survives to use it.” The party survived. The proof has weight.

Phase 10 opens by: surface return. The Yeomanry Council, Iren, Aldea, Vetch, and possibly even some of the Greyhawk Watch must now be confronted with what the party found. This is a phase of political consequences. The descent will need to be re-entered later, with allies and preparation, and the route may have moved on by then.

Outcome H — Prisoner Complication

Triggers: The party crossed the gate (any method) with Haskur, Kethren, Korre, Savax, or Merrit alive in their custody.

Summary: The party has a major NPC complication moving with them into Phase 10. The NPC will:

  • If Merrit: a profound moral and political asset. Phase 10 must address what they do with her safely.
  • If Haskur: a wanted recovery asset. The route will want him back. Other surface forces (Yeomanry intelligence, Greyhawk Watch) want him too. The party is between competing claims.
  • If Kethren: a courier with route authority. Useful but dangerous. He will use any opportunity to bargain his way out of their custody.
  • If Korre: a surface handler whose information could collapse the conspiracy in Greyhawk City. The route will try to silence her.
  • If Savax: a procedural specialist who can navigate any waystation. The route will not let him be casually held; the deeper authority will issue formal recovery orders for one of its own.

Phase 10 opens with: the party managing the prisoner alongside their other goals. The prisoner becomes a Phase 10 plot driver.

Combining outcomes

Outcomes can stack: a party could be in Outcome B + D + H simultaneously (clean opening with alarm, chose escort route, brought Merrit out). The GM should narrate Phase 10's opening with all stacked outcomes in mind.

Per-PC Thread Light Touches at Phase 9's Close

Phase 9 is not the right phase for major per-PC thread payoffs — Phase 8 did most of that heavy lifting. Phase 9's per-PC moments are light, atmospheric, and forward-looking. The GM should offer them at the Lower Route Fork or shortly after.

Cam (halfling networks)

If Cam read the Witness Tally Fragment and saw the partial halfling cousin names: Cam now has confirmation that the same machinery moved his missing people from Tarnsmere to here. The recovery and supply routes both potentially lead to where they were taken.

Cam reads the lintel marks above the three corridors and stops at the recovery route. He turns to the others. “If they're alive,” he says, “they're down one of these. Not all three. One.” He doesn't say which one he thinks. He doesn't have to. He waits for the party to decide.

Gianni (geographic erasure)

If Gianni has been reading the marks carefully: Gianni now understands the conspiracy's logic completely. Surface erasure was the route's standard practice. Missing spurs, missing surveyors, missing claims — the route has been doing this for a long time.

Gianni stands in the Lower Route Fork chamber for a long count. Then she speaks. “Garrold Eft was right. He just didn't have the words. They erased him because he was finding out the things they erase.” She looks at the recovery route lintel. “I want to know what they did with his record below.”

Kitty (chthonic recognition)

If Kitty's chthonic recognition has been continuing through Phase 9: the recognition is stronger at the Lower Route Fork than anywhere else so far. The route's stones know her in a way the surface stones did not.

Kitty places her palm flat against the stone of the Lower Route Fork chamber's wall, briefly. She withdraws it. She does not look at the others. The chamber's air is silent enough that the small click of her teeth meeting is audible. She says, very quietly: “It's louder here.” She does not explain what is louder.

Alicia (pact silence)

Alicia's pact-mark has been cold since the Phase 8 Stair Gallery. At the Lower Route Fork: the mark is no longer just cold. It is reading. Whatever has been receiving her pact has gone silent, but something else, here, has begun paying attention.

Alicia cups her right wrist with her left hand. The pact-mark is no longer cool. It is not warm. It is reading. She thinks: something here has noticed me; not the patron who has been silent; something else. She does not tell the others. She tucks the wrist into her sleeve.

Selvara (stone dreams)

If Selvara's dream-direction has been guiding her: the three corridors register differently in her dream sense. Recovery feels “wrong.” Escort feels “known.” Supply feels “hidden.” The dream that led her here has not ended; it now offers a triage.

Selvara stands at the centre of the chamber and closes her eyes. She does not need to look at the lintels; she can feel the three corridors as three different directions in her dream's geometry. She opens her eyes. She does not say which corridor her dream is asking for. She is, for the first time in the campaign, choosing.

Elle (the iron key)

If Elle used her key in Phase 8 and her order is now formally aware: a letter from her abbess may have reached her via Caelith's pre-descent packet (slipped in among the route-cleaner papers her party recovered above). The letter is brief: “We have read the old records. We will hold the descent for you. Come back if you can. We are still here.”

Elle has the letter folded in her belt pouch. She does not read it again at the Lower Route Fork. She has read it three times already. The third time, she folded it and put it away. Below, here, the letter does not matter mechanically. Below, here, the letter matters because she has it.

For PCs without a named thread

Any PC without a tracked thread should still get a small closing beat at the Fork. Suggested prompts:

  • Fighter / paladin / barbarian: “You weigh your blade. It has not grown lighter. You set your shoulder against whichever of the three you will walk into.”
  • Wizard / sorcerer: “You feel the residual route-warding in the chamber's stones. It is not a spell. It is, as the route is, a procedure. You think about that.”
  • Cleric: “You think a brief silent prayer. It does not arrive at any god you have prayed to. You suspect this is normal for the chamber.”
  • Bard / rogue: “You notice that the three corridors are silent in different ways. Recovery is silent like a sleeping room. Escort is silent like an empty hallway. Supply is silent like the inside of a sealed box. You file the distinction.”
  • Any PC: “You take one slow breath. The air is cold and dry and full of nothing. You walk forward.”

Phase 10 Setup

Phase 10 follows one of the three route branches (or starts with surface return per Outcome G). The phase's specific shape depends on which branch the party committed to at the Lower Route Fork.

Working titles for Phase 10

Choose one based on the chosen branch and tonal preference:

  • “The Witness Road” — emphasises the route's witness procedure; good for Escort Route.
  • “The Black Ledger Road” — emphasises the procedural machinery; good for Supply Route or any path with high evidence focus.
  • “The Recovery Route” — literal naming; good for Recovery Route.
  • “Where Names Are Burned” — emphasises identity erasure; good for any path with thematic focus on the campaign's identity horror.

Phase 10 purpose

  • Follow one of the lower-route branches deeper into the under-road.
  • Begin exposing deep logistics beyond the first waystation.
  • Show the machinery that received surface orders.
  • Push Kethren's status, Haskur's recoverability, and Blooming Rot's true testing function forward.
  • Still do not reveal the final deep faction unless the DM deliberately chooses to accelerate.

Phase 10 beginning options

A. Haskur Transfer Route (Recovery)

Best for: parties pursuing the recovered asset.

Phase 10 opens at a specialised processing waystation, where recovered surface assets are held, classified, and prepared for further use. The party finds:

  • Where Haskur was taken (if alive) or what was done with his record (if dead).
  • The recovery infrastructure at scale — not just Haskur, but other recovered assets the route has processed.
  • The first hints of who or what these recoveries serve.

Combat / encounter design: defensive route personnel; specialised assessment chambers; possible direct confrontation with Haskur or his substituted witness.

B. Kethren Escort Route

Best for: parties pursuing route culture.

Phase 10 opens on the route proper — the long under-road that connects waystations. The party walks the route. They meet:

  • Other couriers like Kethren, of varying ranks.
  • Travelers being escorted between waystations.
  • Route-cleaners doing maintenance work on milestones and marker walls.
  • The fear-of-recall pattern in route personnel becomes very clear.

This route leads, eventually, to a courier-tier waystation — the route's middle administrative office, where dispatches are sorted and forwarded. This is the cleanest path for revealing route culture without yet meeting the deepest figure.

C. Supply Recovery Route

Best for: parties pursuing operational mechanics.

Phase 10 opens at a logistics waystation specialising in cargo. Crates of Blooming Rot samples, recovered material from various surface operations, and bonded claim references all converge here. The party finds:

  • The full scope of the recovery infrastructure.
  • How surface bonded-claim filings pair with planned crop damage in operational practice.
  • The financial machinery of the conspiracy from the route's side.
  • Inventory records that name specific surface farms, families, and (heartbreakingly) specific people whose deaths were planned.

Combat / encounter design: warehouse-style chambers with stacked crates; route logistics personnel; possible discovery of unaccounted material that suggests an even deeper operation.

D. Surface Retreat and Return

Best for: parties choosing Outcome G — retreat with proof.

Phase 10 begins above. The party returns to Caelith, Iren, Aldea, Vetch, or Greyhawk City with evidence. Phase 10 is:

  • A surface investigation phase, where the party uses Phase 9 evidence to confront surface authorities, expose surface fronts, and prepare allies.
  • Political: the Yeomanry Council, the Watch, the Guild, the C&V residual leadership all react.
  • A bridge phase: at the end of Phase 10, the party re-enters the descent — better prepared, with allies, but facing a route that has had weeks to relocate and close.

This option is mechanically slower but politically richer. It is the path for tables that want to play the surface consequences before going deeper.

Hard constraints for Phase 10 player-facing text

Phase 10 inherits all of Phase 8 and Phase 9's hard constraints:

  • Do not reveal the full conspiracy.
  • Do not name Eclavdra.
  • Do not name House Eilserv.
  • Do not name the Vault.
  • Do not use the word Underdark in player-facing text.
  • Do not confirm the “E.” writer is non-human.
  • Do not fully explain Kethren's origin.
  • Do not make Haskur, Kethren, V. Korre, or Route Warden Savax the mastermind.
  • Do not make Phase 10 a random monster gauntlet.
  • Do not dead-end the adventure if the party misses a clue. Every Phase 10 path should self-correct.

What Phase 10 must preserve

The campaign's core horror: systems, permissions, records, recoverability, and respectable surface institutions feeding something older below. Phase 10 should sharpen this. Specifically:

  • The party should leave Phase 10 understanding that the surface conspiracy is the visible tip of a long-running operation.
  • The party should encounter at least one piece of evidence in Phase 10 that connects the route's procedures to the Aerdy Commercial-Court Network's older administrative roots.
  • The party should feel that they are known to the route now — their faces, their names, their procedural classifications.

Phase 10 in three words

Deeper. Known. Categorised.

Phase 9 was bureaucracy in stone. Phase 10 is the road that bureaucracy was a means of administering. The party will spend Phase 10 learning that they have a category now too.
